merge_policy.threshold: merge documents when TF-IDF cosine similarity >= 0.60 OR 5-token shingle containment of the smaller document >= 0.60
merge_policy.why_containment_is_used: Containment catches shorter PDFs that are substantially embedded inside longer PDFs even when whole-document Jaccard/cosine is lower.

documents.title: www.additudemag.com/download/
documents.full_text:
www.additudemag.com/download/
E
motional outbursts are habitual responses to uncomfort-
able feelings. Since the ADHD brain is wired for intense 
feelings, anger and frustration often ambush when adults with 
ADHD underestimate their ability to cope with stressors. 
Metacognition is an awareness of your thought processes 
and patterns. This executive function can help you monitor 
and evaluate your thoughts and behaviors without 
judgment when experiencing anger, thereby changing your 
emotional reactivity. 
You can’t control stressful situations, but you can control 
your reactions by building metacognition skills.
Metacognition Questions
•	 What is happening to activate me or make me feel on 
edge (e.g., receiving negative feedback, being blamed 
unfairly, being chastised, or feeling shame)?
•	 What sensations do I feel when I become angry or upset 
(e.g., headache, stomachache, sweating, chest tightness)?
•	 What behaviors do I resort to when I’m upset or angry 
(e.g., lashing out, attacking, shutting down, catastrophiz-
ing, blaming, or denying)?
•	 How are others reacting to my words and actions? What 
are their faces or bodies showing? 
•	 What has helped me calm down in the past? (Make a list 
of coping tools and save them on your phone and on 
sticky notes around your home). 
How to Respond When You’re…
Mildly Uncomfortable
•	 Notice the negative, critical voice changing your thoughts. 
•	 Compare your limiting beliefs about the situation or 
yourself to the real facts. 
Activated
•	 Acknowledge and validate your feelings. Say, “It’s okay to 
be angry. But what can I do to settle down?”
•	 Repeat affirmations or supportive phrases like, “This 
is not the most fun or my best moment, but I’ll get 
through it.”
•	 Visualize yourself settling down. Picture yourself by a 
lake, tossing in rocks. As each rock falls, feel yourself relax 
into your feet or chair.
On High Alert
•	 Try triangle breathing: Breath in four counts; hold for four 
counts; exhale for six counts. Pause and repeat.
•	 Physically remove yourself from the stressor or 
situation.
•	 Engage in physical activity (e.g., walk, run, or swim).
•	 Turn to a timed distraction. Listen to music or play a 
game on your phone.
Copyright © 2023 by ADDitude, All rights reserved.
ANGER MANAGEMENT
Steps & Scripts to 
Use When You’re 
Fired Up
By Sharon Saline, Psy.D.

ourspace_shared_life_filter.title: Anger Management Steps And Scripts
ourspace_shared_life_filter.shared_facing_summary: Emotion work starts with the body and access needs. Hunger, pain, oxygen, sleep attacks, MCAS reactions, trauma cues, and exhaustion count.
tiny_steps: Check body basics: breathing, pain, food/water, meds, temperature, sensory load.
Lower the demand by one size.
Choose soothe, solve, pause, or ask for help.

flare_day_versions.minimum: One breath, one label, one safe message, or one checkbox.
flare_day_versions.medium: Two to five minutes with everything reachable from bed/chair.
flare_day_versions.full: Use the original worksheet/handout when both bodies and brains have capacity.

accessibility_modifications: Allow anger and grief without turning them into shame.
relationship_and_caregiver_notes: William's needs and Jasper's capacity both count.
Ask before helping; ask before escalating; name the smallest next step.
Pause means pause, not abandonment. I am coming back. I care about us. I need a smaller next step.
What is needed, what capacity exists, what can be smaller, and what can wait?
identity_and_trauma_filter: Use William's masculine language and Jasper's they/them language.
Use consent-based, non-shaming, body-neutral language unless a person has explicitly chosen a term.
Do not turn symptoms, dysphoria, trauma responses, clutter, fatigue, or emotional intensity into moral failure.
avoid_or_adjust: Do not demand immediate calm during respiratory distress, pain flares, dissociation, or overload.

ourspace_shared_revision_metadata.copyright_note: Original source text preserved from user-provided files; added OurSpace accessibility/life-filter metadata for private use.
